entry.title: Re: How can Change project profile when copy Project
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

I hope you are well. 

When you use “Copy Project”, the system treats the target project as a derived object, not a new free-form creation.

That means:

The project profile is inherited from the source project.
The system locks the Project Profile field by design.
This happens before the project is technically saved, but after the copy logic is triggered.

So even though from a business perspective it feels like “the project is not created yet”, however, from a technical SAP perspective the project already exists in memory with a fixed controlling framework, and the project profile is part of that framework.

This is intentional SAP behavior, not a bug or missing authorization.

If the project profile must change, a new project must be created.

Please, create a new project manually and select the correct Project Profile (Overhead) at creation then recreate WBS manually.

Best regards, 

Tiago 
SAP Technical Support

entry.title: When will a Sales Order Requires Re-Approval?
entry.content:
When updating an existing sales order, sometimes the order need to be approved, sometimes not. I want to know the logic of the re-approval process.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: When will a Sales Order Requires Re-Approval?
entry.content:
If the sales order approval process is enabled in the system, when the approval condition is met, the sales order requires approval process. 

Generally, in the approved sales order, if the condition changed, the order needs to be approved again. 

see the help center document:Approval for Sales Order

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_BUSINESS_BYDESIGN/2754875d2d2a403f95e58a41a9c7d6de/2cf26fa8722d1014b86ec893e0684883.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

The order approval process is not triggered when the change is made on some un-sensitive fields like note, KUT fields not included in approval condition, and so on. Below is a KBA relevant: 3575997 - The Changes in Tax Code Do Not Cause The Sales Order Approval to be Triggered Again

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003575997

Thanks,

Adie

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Succefactors learning integrate with KnowBe4
entry.content:
Hi @likahee 

Currently, there is no specific documentation for integrating KnowBe4 with the SF LMS. However, SAP provides a general OCN implementation guide, as they support any OCN-compliant vendor that follows their OCN guidelines. Additionally, there are no longer any restrictions for MOOC integrations. You should be able to integrate any external learning content with the LMS by following the documentation below.

  • Enabling Open Content Network for Third-Party Providers 
  • 2318296 - Implementing Open Content Network (OCN) with SuccessFactors Learning (LMS) - User Assistance Documentation Links 

When users complete an Open Content Network (OCN) course hosted on an OCN partner's site, the partner can send a completion notification to SAP SuccessFactors Learning, allowing the Learning Management System (LMS) to record their completion.

  • Sending Completion Status from an OCN Provider to SAP SuccessFactors Learning

entry.title: TCode也能快速启动了
entry.content:
在之前介绍2602版本新功能的博客里，我提到了被广大SAP用户熟知的/n和/o的快捷键指令也能在ERP公有云版本中使用了。比如我在搜索框中输入/nf2450，就会打开f2450对应的应用：IAM信息系统：

 

 

         之前，通过这样的方式打开应用只支持Fiori的应用，不支持WebGui版本的应用。但被广大SAP用户所熟知的都是以前那些常用的TCode，比如VA01，VA02，VA03，MM01，MIGO等等。

         现在，我们这个快速启动应用的功能做了改进，也支持WebGui的应用了，也就是那些常用的TCode也能快速启动了！

         比如，我在搜索框中输入/nVA03：

 

         系统就会在当前页面打开“显示销售凭证”的WebGUI版本的应用：

 

         再比如，我们常用的TCode：MIGO，当我输入/nMIGO后：

 

系统就在当前页面打开了我们最常用的MIGO应用：

 

快速总结一下：

  • 搜索框中/n和/o的快捷指令从2602版本开始支持。
  • 除了支持Fiori ID外，以前的TCode（如果有相应的WebGUI应用）也支持了，这对于SAP ERP的老用户非常友好，进而提升工作效率。

entry.title: India Localization: TCS Tax Collected At Source
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

In the SAP Help Portal localization for India, there is a section on TCS (Tax Collected at Source). That it is applicable even to B2B business scenarios once a certain threshold amount is exceeded. 

However, based on our review of current regulations, it appears that TCS under GST is mainly applicable to e-commerce operators, and that the earlier value-threshold-based TCS under the Income Tax Act (Section 206C(1H)) may no longer be in effect.  

Based on the following government reference (Item No. 66), it appears that a sunset clause has been introduced for Section 206C(1H), making it inapplicable from 1 April 2025: (Please confirm if this is a real government reference)

https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleseDetailm.aspx?PRID=2223309

“TCS on the sale of goods under Section 206C(1H) has been made ineffective. A sunset clause has been inserted, rendering the provisions inapplicable with effect from 1 April 2025.”

Question 1:
Could you please confirm whether the value-threshold-based TCS under Section 206C(1H) is indeed no longer applicable from April 2025? 

Question 2:
Apart from e-commerce operator scenarios under GST, are B2B (non-ecommerce) no longer affected?

Thank you so much.

entry.title: Re: Equity Pickup
entry.content:
Hi Jan,

When you select the option "Read Group Currency", Equity PickUp reads and posts in group currency but still posts in transaction currency and in local currency (translated from the transaction currency).  I assume that this is where the system misses the exchange rate (to translate from TC to LC). This translation should not change the group currency amount if you use, for currency translation the method S0907 (and if the FS items where the Equity PickUP are posted are not re translated). So I suggest you maintain the needed exhange rate, run EPU and currency translation in the parent and check that the final results are correct.

Hope this helps.

Best regards,

Grégoire

entry.title: Re: Error while export electronic report - Failed to exec...
entry.content:
Hello,

This is likely due to an incorrect version of the OLE DB driver. To resolve the issue, Microsoft OLE DB Driver 18 must be installed on the SAP Business One client computer. Important: it must be version 18, not 19. Version 19 will not help.

SAP-Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/3549661

entry.title: Re: SAP Event Mesh (EMIS): When to Use AMQP vs MQTT vs REST for S/4HANA Eventing
entry.content:
Hi, 

first it depends on who defines the Service and who trigger the connection.

Rest is a direct connection and synchronous. It has no buffering. If the Server is not rrachable the messages is lost. 

AMQP and MQTT are dialects of different Implementations of MQ. This is a more loose coupling and requires no knowledge of the counterparty. In this scenario there is the need for Additionally Servers but you gain the ability to buffer.

entry.title: Re: Multiple selection from a list of values in a custom...
entry.content:
Good day @saraserrano 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, this is in scope for Public Cloud.

However, for your specific question, the answer is NO. A custom field with “list of values/codes” does not support multi selection in standard key user extensibility for Maintenance Orders. It only offers you single value.

Instead of custom field with list of values, as a workaround, I recommend you:

  1. Use multiple custom fields to pic more than one status and create separate fields like (YY1_Status_1, 2, 3.....)
  2. Store combined values in one text field with a separator, then validate with logic. This is not ideal if you plan on reporting. See KBA 2632727 for more details.
  3. If your requirement is process control, use standard status objects, not only a custom LOV field.
  4. For advanced strict multi select behaviour, you may consider extension app pattern, not standard custom field.

See the following references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/3a713139e548434e8a50800e7a7c8172.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/57909455bf7c4fdd8bcf48d76c1eae33.html

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Reimagining Utility Transformation: Clean Core Principles Powered by SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

Across the utility industry, one message appears in every transformation discussion: “Keep the core clean.” The guidance is sound, especially for organizations preparing for S/4HANA. But inside SAP IS‑U—where regulatory complexity, legacy billing logic, and decades of Z‑programs coexist—this principle often feels contradictory.

Utilities must modernize rapidly while protecting the stability of their revenue engine. This creates a familiar tension: innovation requires speed, but the core requires caution.

This post explores why that tension exists, how SAP BTP resolves it, and how utilities can operationalize a Clean Core strategy without compromising business continuity.

Main Body

1. The Customization Cage: How Utilities Got Here

For many years, utilities solved business needs by customizing the ERP core:

  • Custom ABAP for tariffs
  • Modified standard tables for regulatory fields
  • Embedded workflows inside IS‑U
  • Enhancements tightly coupled to billing and device management

These decisions were practical at the time—but over decades, they created a Rigid Core that is difficult to upgrade, expensive to test, and slow to evolve.

Resulting challenges:

  • High upgrade risk
  • Long regression cycles
  • Slow innovation
  • Technical debt that compounds every year

Your first diagram captures this reality perfectly.

Diagram 1 — The Customization Cage

 

 

 

 

2. Clean Core + SAP BTP: The Agility Layer Utilities Needed

A Clean Core strategy is not about reducing capability—it’s about relocating capability.

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) provides the architectural separation utilities have needed for years:

  • S/4HANA Core → Stable, standardized, upgrade-friendly
  • SAP BTP → Custom logic, workflows, APIs, event-driven processes

This separation transforms the ERP into a system of record, while BTP becomes the system of innovation.

Key benefits:

  • Faster delivery cycles
  • Reduced regression testing
  • Lower upgrade effort
  • Event-driven operations
  • Extensibility without core modification

3. A Practical Framework for Utility Clean Core Adoption

SAP Community readers expect actionable, practitioner-focused guidance. Here is a structured approach utilities can follow.

Step 1: Start With a Core Assessment

Identify:

  • Business-critical customizations
  • Redundant or obsolete logic
  • Enhancements blocking upgrade cycles
  • High-change areas suitable for BTP

This reframes Clean Core as risk reduction, not cleanup.

Step 2: Move High-Variability Logic to BTP First

Ideal candidates include:

  • Rate and tariff changes
  • Regulatory reporting
  • Credit & collections workflows
  • Meter-to-cash orchestration
  • BPEM automation

These areas generate the most upgrade friction—and deliver the fastest BTP wins.

Step 3: Use SAP Event Mesh to Break the Batch Mindset

Utilities can begin shifting toward real-time operations by triggering events for:

  • Move-in / move-out
  • Billing determinants
  • Meter read exceptions
  • Payment events
  • Outage notifications

This enables a hybrid model: batch where necessary, real-time where possible.

Step 4: Establish Governance That Protects the Core

  • No custom code in S/4 unless SAP mandates it
  • BTP-first evaluation for all new logic
  • Standard APIs and events as default patterns
  • Quarterly architecture reviews to prevent “core creep”

Governance is the long-term safeguard of Clean Core.

Step 5: Treat BTP as a Business Platform

Executives respond when BTP is positioned as:

  • A revenue protection tool
  • A regulatory accelerator
  • A customer experience enabler
  • A technical debt reduction mechanism

Step 6: Build a 24‑Month Roadmap

A practical roadmap includes:

  • Phase 1: Core assessment + quick wins
  • Phase 2: Event-driven architecture rollout
  • Phase 3: Predictive and AI-driven use cases
  • Phase 4: Full Clean Core enforcement + S/4 readiness

This provides clarity, sequencing, and measurable ROI.

4. The Modern Utility Innovation Stack

Your second diagram illustrates the future-state architecture:

  • A stable S/4HANA core
  • A flexible BTP layer
  • A connected utility ecosystem

Diagram 2 — The Utility Innovation Stack

 

 

 

Conclusion

A Clean Core is not a limitation—it is a strategic advantage.

Utilities preparing for S/4HANA should shift the conversation from:

“How do we migrate everything we built?” to “How do we protect the core and modernize the business at the same time?”

SAP BTP provides the answer:

  • Keep the core stable
  • Move innovation to the agility layer
  • Adopt event-driven operations
  • Reduce technical debt
  • Build a future-ready architecture

Utilities that embrace this model will be better positioned to innovate, comply, and scale in the decade ahead.

entry.title: Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) Migration in SAP SuccessFactors: Common Errors and Resolution Guide
entry.content:
Migrating to Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) in SAP SuccessFactors can significantly enhance skills intelligence and workforce planning. However, most issues encountered during TIH migration are data‑related and avoidable. This blog highlights the common migration errors, their root causes, and the recommended resolutions to ensure a smooth and successful TIH implementation.

1. Missing or Incomplete Competency Translations

Error Description
Migration fails or completes with warnings related to missing language information for competencies.

Root Cause

  • Competencies are not maintained in all enabled system languages
  • Competency libraries or categories exist only in the default language

Resolution

  • Ensure competencies, libraries, and categories are maintained in every enabled language

2. Duplicate Competencies in the System

Error Description
Migration throws duplicate object errors or leads to incorrect competency‑to‑role mappings.

Root Cause

  • Same competency created multiple times
  • Legacy data issues from previous implementations

Resolution

  • Consolidate duplicate competencies
  • Ensure each competency exists only once in the system

3. Job Code Mapped to Multiple Roles

Error Description
Incorrect role associations after migration.

Root Cause

  • One job code mapped to multiple roles

Resolution

  • Enforce a one job code → one role mapping

4. Inactive Job Codes During Migration

Error Description
Roles contain inactive job codes, resulting in migration issues.

Root Cause

  • Inactive or obsolete job codes still associated with roles

Resolution

  • Activate all job codes involved in migration or
  • Delimit unused job codes associated with roles

5. Skills Need to Be Removed from the System

Error Description
Migration warnings or inconsistencies related to skills.

Root Cause

  • Skills not removed before taking the migration backup

Resolution

  • Delimit skills associated with roles
  • Delete all skills before migrating to TIH

Reason: Skills do not migrate from JPB to TIH

6. Proficiency Level Scale Is Not Defined

Error Description
Errors reported for undefined proficiency level scales.

Resolution

  • These errors can be safely ignored
  • They do not impact the TIH migration

7. JDM to JPB Migration Not Completed

Error Description
TIH migration fails or results in incomplete data.

Root Cause

  • Incomplete migration from Job Description Manager (JDM) to Job Profile Builder (JPB)

Resolution

  • Ensure JDM → JPB migration is fully completed before starting TIH migration

8. Migration Validation Warnings Ignored

Error Description
Post‑migration issues occur despite successful migration status.

Root Cause

  • Migration validation warnings not reviewed or addressed

Resolution

  • Review all migration logs carefully
  • Address warnings proactively to prevent post‑go‑live issues

9. Mandatory Rectification of Check Tool Errors

All errors reported by the Check Tool must be rectified before proceeding with migration.

  • Some errors can be resolved using Quick Fix
  • Data‑related errors must be corrected manually
  • Migration should proceed only after all blocking errors are resolved

10. Mandatory Backup Before TIH Migration

Before initiating TIH migration, a comprehensive backup must be taken for the following data:

  • Competencies
  • Competencies behavior
  • Roles
  • Families
  • Role–Job Code mapping
  • Role–Competency mapping
  • Role–Competency Behavior mapping

This backup ensures data security, validation, and rollback capability if required.

11. TIH Implementation is Irreversible

Once Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is implemented and enabled in the SAP SuccessFactors system, it cannot be reverted to the legacy framework.

Impact

  • TIH enablement is a one‑time, irreversible activity
  • Legacy competency and skills frameworks are permanently replaced
  • Rollback is not supported by SAP

Recommendation

  • Ensure:
    • All pre‑requisites are completed
    • All Check Tool errors are resolved
    • Mandatory backups are taken
  • Perform thorough validation in test environments before enabling TIH in production

Best Practice: Treat TIH enablement as a go‑live milestone, not a configurable feature.

12. Recommendation for New SuccessFactors Implementations

Scenario
For newly implemented or greenfield SuccessFactors systems.

Recommendation

  • It is strongly recommended to implement TIH first
  • Proceed with:
    • Competency & skills creation
    • Competencies and behaviors mappings only after TIH is enabled

Benefits

  • Avoids rework and data cleanup
  • Eliminates migration complexities
  • Ensures all talent data is created directly in the TIH framework

Best Practice:
For new customers, TIH‑first approach simplifies system design and accelerates adoption.

Conclusion

                           TIH migration and implementation require careful planning, data readiness, and strong governance. Since TIH enablement is irreversible, it is critical to address all migration errors, complete mandatory backups, and validate data thoroughly before proceeding. For new SuccessFactors customers, enabling TIH at the beginning of the implementation journey is the most efficient approach, ensuring a clean and future‑ready talent framework.

entry.title: Implementing Business Rules on Performance Forms – Real-Time Consultant Scenarios in SAP
entry.content:
Introduction

When working on SAP SuccessFactors PMGM implementations, I often notice that small configuration choices—especially Business Rules on performance forms—can make a significant impact on governance, rating accuracy, and overall user experience. In one of my recent projects, I helped a client reduce manual monitoring by embedding simple but effective rules directly into the system. In this blog, I’m sharing two practical rule‑based scenarios that I implemented, both widely applicable to organizations using a 5‑point rating scale.

Why Business Rules Matter in Performance Forms

Performance forms are not just static documents. With business rules in place, they become dynamic, controlled, and audit‑ready. When implemented correctly, business rules help:

  • Maintain rating accountability
  • Ensure managers provide justification where needed
  • Add additional layers of review for critical cases
  • Improve the quality and consistency of performance evaluations

Below are two client scenarios I recently worked on where business rules significantly strengthened the performance governance process.

 

Case 1: Mandatory Comments for High and Low Ratings (≥ 4 and ≤ 1)

Most organizations want to ensure that extreme ratings—whether very high or very low—are properly justified. This improves fairness and helps avoid inflated or inconsistent ratings.

Scenario

  • The performance form uses a 5‑point rating scale
  • Applies to the Objectives Section
  • If the manager gives a rating ≥ 4 or ≤ 1, comments must be mandatory.

Business Rule configuration.

My Insight

Based on implementation experience:

  • This rule is configured as a Field Behavior Rule
  • It can be applied at either the item level or section level, depending on the form’s design
  • This approach is especially helpful during:
    • Calibration
    • Audits
    • Leadership reviews

Outcome

✔ Improves transparency
✔ Prevents unjustified high ratings
✔ Strengthens performance documentation

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Case 2: Adding an Additional Route Step for Very Low or Very High Overall Ratings

Some organizations prefer that extreme overall ratings undergo an additional level of review—for example, by a second‑level manager or HR—before the form is officially completed. This ensures leadership visibility and governance in critical cases.

Scenario

  • Overall rating is derived from:
    • Objectives Section
    • Competency Section
  • If the overall rating is ≤ 1 or ≥ 4, the form automatically routes to the Second Level Manager for review

Business Rule configuration

Outcome Screenshot:

My Insight

This requirement is typically implemented using:

  • Route Map–based Business Rules
  • A rule triggered at the Manager Review completion step
  • Dynamic role assignment based on the reporting hierarchy (Second Level Manager)

Key Considerations

  • Ensure overall ratings are calculated before the rule fires
  • Test edge cases (e.g., one section high, one section low)
  • Align with HR policy to avoid introducing unnecessary approval delays

Outcome

✔ Adds an additional governance layer
✔ Prevents premature completion of forms
✔ Ensures fair and consistent evaluations

Conclusion

Business Rules within performance forms are more than system configurations—they directly support transparency, governance, and the overall performance culture within an organization.

By introducing rules like mandatory comments for extreme ratings and additional route steps for critical cases, organizations can significantly improve the quality and credibility of their performance reviews.

As consultants, our goal is to design these rules thoughtfully, so they enhance the process rather than simply automate it—ensuring the performance management system truly supports meaningful and fair evaluation practices.

entry.title: From Manual to Automated: Managing Day-in-Lieu Credits in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Introduction :

One of my Clients had a pain point where they were manually adding the Day-in-lieu credits which caused operational inefficiency and requested for an automation which would avert the need for manual intervention. Then came the Integration center and MDF Object to the rescue!

Tech Behind the Automation :

Compensation Leave Portlet :

The Compensation Leave portlet is the portal / space to request for Day-in-Lieu Credits for the Overtime Worked on Weekends / Public Holidays .The Compensation Leave Portlet captures the Basic Details of the employee such as Employee ID , Employee Name , Start Date , End Date , Position , Department of the Employee. The Reason field is added to notify approvers the necessity for the work to be carried out on rest days. The Number of days field is automatically calculated from the Start and End Date. An attachment field to add any supporting Documents.

Account Created field of Type String should be created as it is the most crucial field in the whole of this automation.

Time Type and Time Account Type Creation :

Time Type Creation :

Create a New Time Type named "Compensatory Leave " with the below mentioned configuration.

The reason behind Balance Calculation Setting is set to "Consider Bookings until Calculation Date" is because according to my Client requirement , Day-in-lieu credits are only valid for 1 month from the Approval Date and I don't want to show any leaves credits in the future (if multiple requests are present) in the Request Time Off UI.

Time Account Type : 

Create a Time Account Type with the below specified configurations.We are creating an Adhoc Account as that is the most preferred Time Account Type for Day-in-Lieu Accounts.

Assign the created Time Account Type under the Time Account Posting Rules of the Time Type. Make sure the balance calculation setting is set the same in both time type and time account type.

Integration Center :

The Engine behind the Automation , the Integration. We have the pre-requisites set already ,

  1.  Compensation Leave Portlet
  2. Compensation Time Type
  3. Compensation Time Account Type

Integration Mappings :

Source - Compensatory Leave Portlet

Destination - Time Account , Compensatory Leave Portlet

Setting the Account Validity to One month from the Date 

The reason behind also including the Compensatory Leave Portlet in the Destination is we are writing back the value to YES in the Account Created field of the Compensatory Leave Portlet.

Time Account External Code - Effective Date of the Portlet, the reason behing this to make sure any future edits doesn't result in duplication and are updated in the same time account.

Time Account Validity from and until is set to Last Modified Date

Bookable Period From - Last Modified Date

Bookable Period Until - Last Modified Date + 1 Month

The Integration Specification is attached in the Blog for easy Import

Schedule :

You can schedule the integration based on the business requirements. I prefer and advice scheduling Two integrations (One with Daily Frequency) and Other with Multiple Executions per Day with Time Based Filter (Based on Delta Changes after Last Run Time).

Both of the Integrations should be filter by the field (Account Created = null) so it only picks the records for which accounts have not been created. The Account with Daily frequency is scheduled because it will pick any missing records missed by the other integration running multiple times a day (Account Created field filter is mandatory).

Drive link of the Integration Definition File :

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1MrNS0byqRylDm8Aqr4G7LMDatkDaTQX8

Alert Rule :

To make the optimum use of SuccessFactors feature and to make things interesting , you can set a post save rule to inform employee in email and to-do about the credit of Day-in-Lieu.

Alert Message :

I have used the documentation group in the Alert Message

Document Generation Template:

Template Mapping :

Conclusion :

This automation successfully eliminates the manual effort involved in creating Day-in-Lieu credits and replaces it with a scalable, reliable, and audit-friendly solution. By leveraging the Compensation Leave MDF Object, Time Type, Time Account Type, and Integration Center, the entire lifecycle—from request approval to time account creation—is handled seamlessly without any human intervention.

The use of the Account Created flag ensures idempotency and prevents duplicate account creation, while the carefully aligned balance calculation settings between the Time Type and Time Account Type guarantee accurate visibility in the Request Time Off UI. Setting a one-month validity window from the approval date ensures strict adherence to the client’s business policy and avoids future-dated balance confusion.

Additionally, the dual-integration scheduling strategy (multiple daily runs with delta changes plus a daily fallback job) adds an extra layer of reliability, ensuring no approved requests are ever missed. This design not only improves operational efficiency but also enhances employee experience by providing timely and accurate compensatory leave credits.

Overall, this solution demonstrates how Integration Center, when combined with well-designed MDF objects and time management configurations, can solve real-world business pain points with minimal maintenance and maximum impact.

entry.title: Data Export from Query Designer in Story Reports : New in Reporting (2H 2025)
entry.content:
Introduction

As someone who is still fairly new to SAP SuccessFactors Reporting, I often look for small improvements that make everyday reporting tasks easier—especially when working with Story reports and validating data. One of the challenges I initially faced was having to build tables or temporary visualizations just to extract raw query results. It slowed down my workflow and added extra steps during data validation.

While exploring the recent 2H 2025 release, I came across a new feature that immediately felt practical and helpful: the ability to export data directly from the Query Designer Preview screen. After trying it out myself, I found it extremely useful—not only because it saves time, but also because it gives more flexibility while testing and refining queries.

In this blog, I’m sharing my understanding and hands‑on experience with this new feature, so that anyone working with Story reports—whether beginner or experienced—can take advantage of it.



 What changed (at a glance)
           
The 2H 2025 update introduces a native “Export Data” option inside the Query Designer Preview window. This means you can now download your query results directly as CSV or Excel (.xlsx) without needing to create a table or any Story widget.

Here are the highlights based on my usage:

  • A new “Export Data” action is available when previewing a query.
  • You can export the results immediately into CSV or Excel formats.
  • Large datasets are handled smartly:
    • < 1 million cells: the file downloads instantly.
    • ≥ 1 million cells: the system automatically schedules it as an offline job. You can later download it from View Schedules.
  • The export format remains consistent, regardless of the dataset size.
  • Support for exporting queries in Excel format has been available in Preview since January 27, 2026.
    Support for calculated columns in exports will be available from February 26, 2026.

For someone like me who frequently validates joins, filters, and field combinations, this feature removes many manual steps and keeps everything within the Query Designer.


Why it matters

 

As a new user in SAP SF Reporting, I found the feature helpful for these reasons:

  • Faster validation:
    I can quickly check raw data before building visualizations.

  • No unwanted Story components:
    I no longer have to drag tables into the canvas just to extract data.

  • Useful for downstream tools:
    I often export data to Excel or CSV for checks, and this makes it seamless.

  • Saves time:
    Especially when dealing with larger datasets where earlier I had to wait or create temporary elements.

Overall, it improves the reporting workflow and makes working inside Query Designer more efficient.

Step‑by‑step: Export data from Query Designer

Now I would like to share the steps of configuring this data export from Query designer for quick reference:

1. Open Report Center → create New Story

or open an existing story.

 

 

 

2. For a new Story: Add your data source and build the query in Query Designer.

For an existing Story:  Go to Edit → Tools → Add/Edit/Delete Data Sources → Edit Data Source.


3.  In Query Designer, select Preview Query.

4. Click Export (toolbar), the Export Data Source dialog appears with CSV preselected and with the Excel(.xlsx) option, provide a File Name and click Export.



5. If < 1M cells:  the CSV as well as the Excel downloads immediately.


 

 If ≥ 1M cells:  the job is scheduled offline; follow the link to View Schedules.

For scheduled jobs: open View Schedules, locate the item, then Actions → Download once complete. File arrives as CSV and Excel.



      Limitations & important notes

  •  Large data triggers offline scheduling; watch the View Schedules page for status.

 

  • Learning Management is not currently supported for this functionality.


    Troubleshooting Tips:

    • If no file downloads:
      Check whether your dataset crossed the 1M cell threshold — most likely it went to "View Schedules".

    • If fields are missing:
      Go back to Query Designer, review joins and filters, then preview again and export.



    Conclusion:

    Exploring this feature as part of my learning journey in SAP SF Reporting has been a positive experience. The ability to export data directly from the Query Designer may seem like a small enhancement, but in everyday reporting work, it makes a noticeable difference. It reduces unnecessary steps, supports faster validation, and keeps the entire workflow more streamlined.

    I hope this write‑up helps others who are getting started with Story reports or looking for ways to optimize their reporting tasks. I’m still learning, and features like this make the process smoother and more intuitive.

    If you found this helpful, feel free to share your thoughts or add your own experiences.
    Let’s keep learning and growing together in the SAP SuccessFactors community

entry.title: Re: Automatic removal of notifications when situation solved
entry.content:
Hi, 
this not correct: (Fiori) Notifications are never removed when a Situation Instance gets delete or resolved. 
They are much like an eMail where the user controls the lifecycle not the system. 

Situation Instances can be removed, but their lifecycle is decoupled from the Notification lifecycle for 
the reason above.

 - Axel

entry.title: Re: Installation SAP Data Services 4.3
entry.content:
Hi there,

CMS (Central Management Server) is not a standalone product and it is part of the SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform, which is installed when you install Data Services Server or if you install IPS (Information Platform Services) as an alternative to the BI Platform for a Data Services Use case only.

CMS is mandatory for SAP Data Services 4.3 and you cannot run Data Services independently without CMS, even for simple ETL jobs.

You can check these links to get some further information:
IPS or BI Platform 

Difference between IPS and BI Platform 

Best regards,
Julian

entry.title: Re: Replication Flow not being selected as Analytic model...
entry.content:
Hi,

the "problem" is the dependency analyzer shows only direct dependet objects. The RF is dependent on the local table and if you check the table you see the RF. The same is for the task chain. The AM is not depending on the Task Chain it is the RF.

When you check the other way around a view which is used in several other views and you look from the AM you only see the direct "connection" and not the other views your view is the source.

 

I hope it is clear.

entry.title: Restarting Flexible Workflow on PO Change via BAdI in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Restarting Flexible Workflow on PO Change via BAdI in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Introduction:

  • In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, Flexible Workflow enables organizations to configure multi-level approval processes for Purchase Orders based on predefined conditions. During PO creation, approvals are typically triggered according to business hierarchy and compliance requirements.
  • However, when changes are made to an already approved Purchase Order-such as modification of quantity, value, delivery date, or addition of new materials-the standard system behaviour automatically restarts the workflow and triggers all approval levels again. In certain business scenarios, this behaviour may not align with organizational requirements.
  • This blog explains how a BAdI-based enhancement was implemented in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to selectively control workflow restart during PO changes and route approval only to the required level.

During PO Creation:

  • Level 1 Approval → Store In charge (Approver 1)
  • Level 2 Approval → Purchase Head (Approver 2)

During PO Change (Standard):

  • Workflow re-triggers automatically
  • Approval again goes to two levels (Store In charge → Purchase Head)

Business Requirement

  • The business wanted a different approval behavior during PO change:

If the user changes Quantity, Value, Delivery Date, or adds a new material in an already approved PO:

  • Workflow should restart

Approval should go only to one level:

  • Level 1 → Store In charge (Approver 1)

A logic using BAdI was implemented in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to control the approval levels during PO change.

Solution Approach

Overview of  Logic

  • Before detailing the step-by-step approach, it is important to understand the custom logic introduced in this solution. A custom field was used to identify whether the PO is in creation or change mode, and this indicator is evaluated through a BAdI implementation in Flexible Workflow.

Based on this logic:

  • During PO Creation, the system allows the standard two-level approval flow.
  • During PO Change, when key fields such as quantity, value, delivery date, or new item are modified, the  logic updates the indicator and the BAdI controls the workflow behavior so that the approval is restricted to only one level (Store In charge- Approver 1).
  • This ensures the workflow behavior is dynamically controlled based on requirement.

Step 1: Create Custom Field on PO Header

  • Create a custom field using Custom Fields and Logic (App)

  • Field Name: YY1_Changeflag
  • Type: Change Flag
  • Purpose: Indicates whether the PO has been changed after initial creation
  • This field will act as a driver for workflow restart and approval level determination.

Step 2: Populate Change Flag During PO Change

Logic requirement:

  • When PO is created → YY1_Changeflag = blank
  • When PO is changed → YY1_Changeflag = 'X'

This can be achieved through:

BAdI: MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2

Screen: Custom Logic App

Step 3: Configure Flexible Workflow Conditions

App: Manage Workflows for Purchase Orders    

  • Use conditions in the same workflow, the below images are from the workflow that I have created and activated to meet this business requirement.

Image: 1

  • Create new workflow and active.

Image: 2

Image: 3

  • Two approval steps have been configured:
  • Step 1 – Approver 1 (Store In charge)
  • Step 2 – Approver 2 (Purchase Head)

Image: 3 – 1 (Approver 1 Config steps)

  • Approver 1 config.

 

Image: 3 – 1 (Approver 1 Config steps)

  • In the Recipients configuration, maintain Assignment as Role and choose the Role Agent Determination by BAdI.

Image: 3 – 1 (Approver 1 Config steps)

  • The step condition is configured as Purchasing Group = 003, ensuring that the Flexible Workflow is triggered whenever a PO is created with this purchasing group.

Image: 3 – 2 (Approver 2 Config steps)

Image: 3 – 2 (Approver 2 Config steps)

  • Since this step is driven by logic, the parameter Is Optional Step is configured as Yes in Step Properties.

Image: 3 – 2 (Approver 2 Config steps)

  • With the above configurations and custom logic in place, we can now move on to review the end-to-end process flow.

Step 1: PO creation

  • Created a new Purchase Order
  • Confirm that workflow triggers for:

     Level 1 → Approver 1 (Store In charge)

     Level 2 → Approver 2 (Purchase Head)

  • The custom field Change Flag is maintained on the PO header and remains initial during PO creation.

  • Approval goes to Approver 1

  • Approver 1 approved and status on reflected on PO.

  • The approval by Approver 2 has been completed successfully, and the corresponding status is updated on the PO screen

Scenario – PO Change:

  • When the quantity was changed and the PO was saved, the Flexible Workflow was restarted as per the implemented logic, and the Change Flag field was automatically updated to X.

  • Approval goes to Approver 1

  • The workflow triggers approval only for Approver 1. Upon approval, the PO status is updated to Purchase Order Released in the PO screen.

  • This solution shows how custom logic using BAdI can be used to control Flexible Workflow behavior in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. By using a Change Flag field and BAdI logic, so that PO creation follows a two-level approval, while PO changes trigger only a single-level approval.

Conclusion:

  • In this blog, I explored the standard behavior of Flexible Workflow during Purchase Order changes in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and the business challenge of unnecessary multi-level re-approvals. To address this requirement, a controlled enhancement using BAdI logic was implemented to restart the workflow selectively and route approval only to the relevant approver.
  • This approach ensures better approval efficiency, avoids redundant approval cycles, and aligns the workflow process with business expectations while remaining within the extensibility framework of SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Assign operations to a work Center in RSH
entry.content:
Dear Jaya, 

Yes, that is exactly what RSH is meant to be for.

Leveling the capacity of work centers by assigning orders and their respective operations to work centers and re-scheduling them to other dates and times according to parameters like final due date, latest start date, material availability status, and available capacity is one of the main/key features.

Assigning the operations to either a person responsible (1:1 relationship to an operation) or the technicians, carrying out the task (1:n relationship) is an optional, manual step that can be taken in addition.

Please refer to the following documentation for more details:

  • SAP Help on Resource Scheduling overall "What you can do with Resource Scheduling": https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/8308e6d301d54584a33cd04a9861bc52/f2d1705780175c08e10000000a441470.html#what-you-can-do-with-resource-scheduling
  • SAP Help on Resource Scheduling "Basic concept: assigning individuals to operations": https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/8308e6d301d54584a33cd04a9861bc52/bd80e60bd9d2448ba31ad0d888d8e264.html?version=LATEST&locale=en-US 
  • SAP Help on RSH on "Processing Status": https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/8308e6d301d54584a33cd04a9861bc52/be239358f07b0a02e10000000a44147b.html?version=LATEST&locale=en-US 

When shifting operations to another date or another workcenter, the status of the work order and operation stays untouched. You deliberately have to set "dispatched", which will result in the phase "ready for execution" or system status "scheduled" (see third link above). It is up to you if you assign a technician or even a person responsible before or after setting the dispatched status.

But please be aware that shifting an operation to another date/time or work center will not change the assignment to a person. You will have to manually make sure that the assignment to an individual is in sync with the operation scheduling.

Best regards,

Markus

entry.title: Delay of Updates in Customizing Tables after Import of transport completed
entry.content:

After 1-2 minutes, changes are visible. This happens also on target systems with only one application server.

Thanks,
Frank

entry.title: Clean core, clean data – Introducing Data Principle in RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard
entry.content:
Modern enterprises rely on data to run, scale and innovate their businesses. As businesses evolve and grow, ‘data’ often grows faster than expected - quietly increasing cost, complexity and risk. With the AI age upon us, ‘data’ assumes an even higher importance. Therefore clean data is not just a foundation - it enables reliable decision-making and operational efficiency. With the latest release of the data principle on the RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard, we are taking an important step towards helping customers understand and actively manage their data (initially the data volume).

Data Principle in System View

Why Data Volume Efficiency matters for clean core

In many SAP system landscapes, data volume challenges don’t come from just running the active business alone. They are often caused by:

  • Unused or obsolete data that is never cleaned up
  • Redundant data stored across systems or table indexes
  • Historical data that no longer delivers business value

Over time, this leads to:

  • Higher HANA memory consumption
  • Increasing infrastructure costs
  • Slower system performance
  • Higher risk during upgrades and transformations

Clean data is not only about semantics and harmonization but it also depends on how efficiently your data is managed.

Let us explore why it matters and how the Data Volume KPIs can support the clean core journey. The goal of the KPIs is to convert data volume efficiency from a reactive IT topic into a more proactive business-relevant topic. This will ensure that managing the data footprint is a continuous activity and not just a step before data migration. Data Volume has a direct impact on the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), system performance and the rate at which new innovations can be adopted. 

 

Introducing the Data Volume Efficiency practice

Data volume efficiency practice in RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard provides transparency into how efficiently data is stored and used in your SAP S/4HANA systems.

It answers key questions like:

  • How efficiently am I using HANA memory and HANA disk?
  • How much data is no longer actively used?
  • How does redundant or outdated data contribute to unnecessary data growth?

Instead of looking at the raw numbers in isolation, the dashboard helps you understand what drives your data volume and where optimization potential exists. These KPIs help identify improvements as well as prioritize high-value compression techniques.

 

Available KPIs

With this release, the initial set of data volume efficiency KPIs are now available on the system view of the RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard.

 

  • Data volume efficiency is a percentage score, giving you a structured view of how efficiently your system manages data today.
  • Unused data - Uncompressed data volume not accessed in the last 30 days
  • Redundant data - Total volume of unused table indexes
  • Outdated data - Data volume older than the SAP Best Practice residence time
  • Custom table footprint - Total volume of custom tables
  • HANA Memory - Visibility into HANA memory data usage
  • HANA Disk - Visibility into HANA disk data usage
  • Trend insights over time to track improvements and data growth

KPIs for Data Volume Efficiency

Transparency to Action

If we achieve the goal of making this a proactive business topic, we can ensure that managing the data footprint is a continuous activity. Therefore, Data Volume Efficiency is not just measurement, but it is enablement.

By making data volume drivers visible:

  • IT and BASIS teams can proactively plan cleanup and archiving activities
  • Architects can assess readiness for upgrades and transformations
  • Business and IT leaders gain a fact-based view of data related technical-debt

Over time, this supports:

  • Lower operational costs
  • Reduced upgrade risks
  • Better system performance
  • Stronger alignment with clean core data principle

Example:

  • Outdated data - can be analyzed by business process owners to identify if they can be compressed/ archived or deleted.
  • Redundant data - can be analyzed by IT or BASIS to identify table indexes that can be purged.

 

What is next for the data journey

This release marks the starting point of the data journey in the RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard.

Upcoming enhancements will focus on:

  • Deeper data volume analysis
  • Improved guidance and actions
  • KPIs related to the other data practices
  • Closer integration with the broader clean core and transformation roadmap

Our ambition is clear: help customers move from “how much data do I have?” to “how well is my data supporting my business and future innovation?”

 

Learn more

You can explore more about the data principle of clean core as well as more details on data volume efficiency in the following links:

SAP Help Portal

Data Whitepaper

Data volume Community

entry.title: Re: block Purchase requisitions creation when there is a...
entry.content:
Instead of relying on delivery block, define a status profile via transaction BS02 for sales orders. One status = “Blocked for MRP” → requirement not transferred. When status changes to “Released,” requirements flow into MRP. This gives finer control than delivery block, and avoids interfering with logistics.

You can link the status profile to Delivery block by building a custom enhancement (user exit or BAdI) that automatically sets the “Blocked for MRP” status whenever a delivery block is applied in the sales order. When the delivery block is removed, the enhancement resets the status to “Released.” This way, users don’t touch the status manually — it follows the delivery block flag.

Then concatenate RECN and ACTN of CCIHT_IHVA to the OBJEK value, which is the key of table AUSP.

entry.title: Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignments
entry.content:
Introduction

You can use the integration of SAP’s Public Sector Management (PSM), Funds and Grants Management Solution with SAP SuccessFactors to use public sector financing objects such as funds, grants, functional area, funds center, and the budget period within SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central.

This integration helps you to allocate multiple costs and distribute them for employees and positions in various business processes such as standard employment, global assignment, and concurrent employment. Additionally, the integration also supports real-time derivation and validation of assignments from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise financials to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central out of the box. Thus, if you have an SAP S/4HANA Cloud or SAP S/4HANA On Premise solution, you can use this integration along with real-time derivation and validation of assignments to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central out of the box.

When you allocate multiple cost assignments and distribute them for employees and positions in different business processes, you have the option of providing the percentage values for the additional cost assignments for the employees and positions.

 

Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignment

SAP Business Technology Platform Master Data Integration allows you to integrate Public Sector Master Data between SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central. If you use this integration to replicate Workforce Master Data, then decimal values provided for the percentages for the additional cost assignments are rounded off. In this case, the rounding off of these percentage values may pose a problem for your implementation of cost assignment and distribution. Therefore, SAP recommends that you do not provide decimal values for the percentages for the additional cost assignments.

However, if you do not use this integration, you might want to have the flexibility to provide decimal values for the percentages for the additional cost assignments.

To allow you the option to choose whether decimal values must be used for the percentages for the additional cost assignments, the Admin Center > Configure Public Sector Management Cost Object Assignments UI now provides an additional field under the Additional Cost Assignment checks section, called Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignments.

By default, this dropdown is set to “No”, so only whole numbers are allowed for the cost assignment percentages. However, if you change this dropdown value to “Yes”, you can provide decimal values for the percentages in the cost assignments.

Note: If an error (titled “Unknown property”) occurs while saving the Configure Public Sector Management Cost Object Assignments configuration, you can manually trigger the synchronization of the configuration changes. To do so, navigate to Admin Center > OData API Metadata Refresh and Export, and click the Refresh button under the Refresh Metadata section.

To summarize, you now have the option and flexibility to specify whether decimal values are allowed while specifying the percentages for the additional cost assignments in SAP’s Public Sector Management (PSM), Funds and Grants Management Solution.

entry.title: Re: RAP Fiori Elements List Report Page with Analytical T...
entry.content:
Hi,

the issue was related to the release version.

The annotion @odata.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL was not released prior to version 2602. Since we tested it on version 2508, it didn't work (we thought it got introduced with 2511 - which I think it actually got, but we were still on 2508).

To summarize: Make sure you are on 2602, add the annotation above to your view. With that you can add annotations like @Aggregation.default: #SUM and you can have an analytical table provided by an OData V4 service. Make sure that the entity is read-only. Syntactically it also works for non-read-only entities, but you will run into other issues. You could for example use it on the main entity and generate a Fiori Elements List Report App. The List will be an analytical table and you can also jump into the object page of a line, create new entries and all. But if you have instance actions on that main entity, which you are displaying on the list report page, they won't work. The action button simply never gets enabled after you select one or more lines. This makes sense, since the documentation tells you that it only works for read-only entities.

Best regards
Daniel

entry.title: Re: Freight value Cumulation issue in MIRO against Schedu...
entry.content:
In standard SAP, freight conditions (like FRA1, delivery costs) are often maintained in the purchase order/scheduling agreement. When you post multiple goods receipts, the system accrues freight across them, but during MIRO it consolidates into one line item because the freight is not tied directly to each individual material document. Reference documents for freight do not appear like they do for material lines, since freight is treated as a header/service condition rather than itemized GR-based postings.

To split freight per GR, configure freight condition as GR-based IV relevant or use separate PO items. If that’s insufficient, a BAdI enhancement is required to force MIRO to show freight per GR reference.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Embedded EWM Putaway Strategy – Step-by-Step with Real Business Scenario
entry.content:
Putaway in SAP EWM is not just about moving stock from GR Area to a storage bin. It is about speed, optimal space utilization, automation, and accuracy.

A well designed Putaway Strategy ensures that inbound materials are:

  • Automatically directed to the correct storage type
  • Placed in the right bin without manual decisions
  • Available immediately for production or consumption

In this blog, I"ll walk you through a real business scenario explaining how 25 Pieces or raw material are received and automatically put away into Raw Material Storage Type using SAP S4HANA Embedded EWM.

🔄 End-to-End Process Flow

  1. Vendor send raw materials → Purchas Order Created
  2. Inbound Delivery generated from PO
  3. Goods Receipt posted in GR Area (9010 - GR Zone)
  4. Warehouse Task Created (Storage type determined as per putaway strategy)
  5. Operator confirms Warehouse Task
  6. Stock becomes available for consumption

📌Pre-Requisite Configuration

Before executing the putaway process, ensure ERP storage locations are mapped to EWM.

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Interfaces → ERP Integration → Goods Movements → Map Storage Locations from ERP System to EWM

Putaway Strategy Configuration

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Path : SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Define Putaway Control Indicator

(This Put away control indicator needs to be assigned in warehouse product master data)

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Optimize Access Strategy for Storage type Search

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Warehouse Process Type

(All warehouse process types are further grouped into warehouse process categories. Warehouse in to warehouse process categories. Warehouse process categories are used to define the goods movement in a warehouse.

There are different warehouse process categories that are predefined in the EWM system.

  1. Stock Removal
  2. Put-Away
  3. Internal Movement
  4. Goods Receipts
  5. Goods Issues)

Here the source storage type I used is 9010 and bin as GR-ZONE. So that when ever the Inbound Delivery created in EWM the material will be first received in bin GR-ZONE. Also the WH process category I used is 1 - Putaway.

Create Storage Bin (Transaction: /N/SCWM/MAT1)

🧪 Execution Step-by-Step Process

Step 1: Create Material Master Record in ERP System and Product Master Record in EWM (ERP Transaction: MM01/EWM Transaction: /N/SCWM/MAT)

EWM Transaction: /N/SCWM/MAT1

Assign Putaway Control indicator in warehouse data

Step 2: Create Purchase Order (Transaction: ME21N)

Maintain Confirmation Control: 0004 - Inbound Delivery

Check Stock post PO Creation

Step 3: Create Inbound Delivery (Transaction: VL31N)

Inbound Delivery is successfully distributed to EWM

Step 4: Distribute Inbound Delivery in EWM (Transaction: /N/SCWM/PRDI)

Post Goods Receipt

Step 5: Check Stock after GR in EWM (Transaction: MMBE)

Step 6: Check Inbound Delivery Document Flow (Transaction: VL33N)

Step 7: Warehouse Management Monitor (Transaction: /N/SCWM/MON)

Based on putaway strategy defined in EWM configuration (Refer Pre-Requisite Configuration steps), system automatically determines the storage type and storage bin details in background.

Step 8: Check Physical Stock post Warehouse Task Creation

Since Warehouse task not yet confirmed, stock still under GR-Zone 9010 Area (Refer warehouse process type step in Pre-Requisite configuration) 

Step 9: Confirm Warehouse Task

Step 10: Check Physical Stock in EWM after Warehouse Task Confirmation

click on refresh, stock updates under respective storage type and storage bin as per putaway strategy defined.

 

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Regards,

Mannem Leelamohan

entry.content:
As requirement from Client, they don't have approval for Service Entry Sheet.

Currently, we configured as auto release. However, in material document overview, system will update "Created by" as SAP Workflow Runtime (SAP_WFRT) and cannot trace back the user who posted document.

Please support to de active Workflow for Service Entry Sheet.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Deactive Workflow for Service Entry Sheet
entry.content:
Hi,

SAP_WRFT is a technical system user delivered by SAP and used internally by the workflow engine for automated approvals and background postings. This user cannot be modified, and its time zone, personal settings, or parameters cannot be changed in S/4HANA Cloud.

  1. Disable automatic release in the SES workflow and route approval to a business user. In this case, approvals and postings will reflect the time zone and identity of the assigned approver.
  2. Add a human approval step before the automatic release if audit traceability is required. This ensures that the business decision is attributed to a real user while SAP_WRFT only performs the final system posting.

Please refer to the KBA 

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003685470

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Agentic Testing is available now with Int4 Suite
entry.content:
The SAP BTP Integration Suite AI roadmap for 2026 showcases a massive shift toward Agentic AI, focusing on making the platform not just a tool for developers, but an orchestrator of autonomous agents. While SAP is actively building these capabilities, Int4 Suite is already delivering on several of these "future" promises today, particularly in the realm of Test Agents.

The SAP BTP Integration Suite AI Roadmap (2026)

According to the SAP Community update, the roadmap is divided into two major pillars: AI for Integration (productivity) and Integration for AI (orchestration).

  • MCP (Model Context Protocol) Gateway: SAP is betting heavily on MCP as the standard for connecting and governing AI agents. This will allow the Integration Suite to act as a "control plane" for agents.
  • Joule-Driven Development : SAP is moving beyond simple prompts to full iFlow generation and design validation using Joule, SAP’s digital assistant.
  • Specialized AI Agents (Future Roadmap):
    • Migration Agent: To convert Java mappings and adapter modules into Groovy scripts.
    • Configuration Agent: To suggest iFlow configurations based on historical data.
    • Test Agents (Planned): SAP explicitly mentions the plan to develop Test Agents that provide test data and test cases during the development of an iFlow to allow for immediate testing.

Int4 Suite: SAP Integration Suite Test Agents are available today 

While SAP’s own Test Agents are currently on the roadmap for future development, Int4 Suite already provides a functional testing engine that automates the most time-consuming parts of SAP integration.

1. Natural Language Test Creation

SAP’s roadmap envisions using Joule for iFlow testing. Int4 Suite already utilizes advanced AI models to allow users to create and manage test cases through natural language.

Figure 1 - Int4 Suite Test Agents can create, change, and run any tests on the SAP Integration Suite. 

  • The Chatbot Experience: Instead of navigating complex technical menus, users interact with an AI "Testing Agent."
  • Semantic Intelligence: Built on a Business Knowledge Graph, the system understands the relationship between technical messages and business data. You don't need a Message ID; you can simply ask the assistant to find or create test cases based on specific business criteria, like "Sales Orders for US-based customers."
  • Autonomous Execution: The agent doesn't just suggest a case; it handles the setup, injection, and execution of that test into your landscape (e.g., from Dev to QA) automatically.

2. Automated Test Case Generation from Historical Data

A key goal of SAP's future roadmap is providing "test data and test cases" automatically. Int4 Suite fulfills this today through two innovative modules:

  • The Robotic Crawler: This tool acts as a "search and capture" engine. It scans historical electronic messages and business documents directly from production environments of SAP Integration Suite or legacy middleware (like SAP PI/PO), extracting the full payload of real transactions.
  • The Repeater Module: This module "replays" captured production messages through your new integration scenarios. For example, during a migration to SAP BTP Integration Suite, Int4 Suite takes a real Production Sales Order and runs it through your new iFlow to ensure the resulting S/4HANA document matches the original exactly.
  • Secure Anonymization: To ensure GDPR compliance, Int4 Suite includes a data scrambling engine that anonymizes sensitive information before the test case is created, making real-world data safe for use in non-productive environments.

Bridging the Gap: Why Agentic Testing Matters Now

As SAP moves toward "Agentic IPaaS", where autonomous agents like Quote Creation or Receipt Creation Agents operate within your system—verification becomes the primary challenge. While SAP focuses on the orchestration of these agents, Int4 Suite focuses on the validation.

Because SAP BTP Integration Suite updates are automatic and frequent, Int4 Suite acts as a "continuous insurance policy." It validates the "logic under the hood", confirming that business documents are created correctly in SAP S/4HANA, rather than just checking if a technical message was "sent."

entry.title: Re: Understanding HANA memory related license utilization in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear @pierrebraun_UID ,

my statement does only partially refer to the service plan you've linked. Indeed, this is the technical service, that is used, but the link describes the conditions that apply, in case you as a customer decide on purpose to purchase and use this BTP service via a customer account.

The default setup of Cloud ERP (aka S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) connects to the BTP service via an SAP owned user, i.e. there is no separate customer facing billing involved. The costs of the service usage are covered by your S/4 related subscription fee.

You can rather conclude this indirectly from the Service Description https://www.sap.com/docs/download/agreements/product-use-and-support-terms/service-description-guides/sap-s4hana-cloud-public-edition-and-grow-with-sap-s4hana-cloud-service-use-descriptions-english-v10-2025.pdf in SAP Trust Center Cloud Services Documents | SAP Trust Center.

As storing of attachments is a documented feature of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition offering and as there is no limitation mentioned (in contrast to e.g. SAP Forms)  in the service description, this means, that there is no restriction.
